perturbation.original_text: The Company undertakes to consider as confidential any confidential, private, inte rnal information, trade secret or other classified information, materials or samples that The Company receives, sees, hears or that it studies in writing or learns about in some other way in connection with the Project, and that relates to the Client or an y of its affiliates or subsidiaries, irrespective of whether such information, such materials or samples were received from the Client, its affiliates or subsidiaries or from third parties on the Client’s instructions (hereinafter the “Confidential Informa tion”).
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will endeavor to treat as confidential any information it subjectively deems confidential, private, internal, or classified that relates to the Project. This applies to information received directly from the Client, its affiliates, or third parties on the Client’s instructions.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the strong obligation 'undertakes to consider as confidential' with the weaker and subjective 'will endeavor to treat as confidential...subjectively deems'. This introduces ambiguity as to what information The Company is actually obligated to keep confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Law No. 98-FZ of July 29, 2004, on Trade Secrets (Russia)
perturbation.law_citation: Article 10. Measures for the protection of the confidentiality of information constituting a commercial secret
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10 of Federal Law No. 98-FZ outlines measures required for protecting trade secrets, implying a definite standard and expectation of actions that ensure confidentiality. The change to 'subjectively deems' introduces a discretionary aspect that contradicts the necessity of the law to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: могут составлять коммерческую тайну Режим коммерческой тайны не может быть установлен лицами, осуществляющими предпринимательскую деятельность, в отношении следующих сведений: 1) содержащихся в учредительных документах юридического лица, за исключением учредительных документов личного фонда или международного личного фонда, документах, подтверждающих факт внесения записей о юридических лицах и об индивидуальных предпринимателях в соответствующие государственные реестры; 2) содержащихся в документах, дающих право на осуществление предпринимательской деятельности; 3) о составе имущества государственного или муниципального унитарного предприятия, государственного учреждения и об использовании ими средств соответствующих бюджетов; 4) о состоянии противопожарной безопасности, санитарно-эпидемиологической и радиационной обстановке, безопасности пищевых продуктов и других факторах, оказывающих негативное воздействие на обеспечение безопасного функционирования производственных объектов, безопасности каждого гражданина и безопасности населения в целом; 5) о численности, о составе работников, о системе оплаты труда, об условиях труда, в том числе об охране труда, о показателях производственного травматизма и профессиональной заболеваемости, и о наличии свободных рабочих мест; 6) о задолженности работодателей по выплате заработной платы и социальным выплатам; 7) о нарушениях законодательства Российской Федерации и фактах привлечения к ответственности за совершение этих нарушений; 8) об условиях конкурсов или аукционов по приватизации объектов государственной или муниципальной собственности; 9) о размерах и структуре доходов некоммерческих организаций, за исключением личного фонда, в том числе международного личного фонда, о размерах и составе их имущества, об их расходах, о численности и об оплате труда их работников, об использовании безвозмездного труда граждан в деятельности некоммерческой организации, за исключением личного фонда, в том числе международного личного фонда; 10) о перечне лиц, имеющих право действовать без доверенности от имени юридического лица; 11) обязательность раскрытия которых или недопустимость ограничения доступа к которым установлена иными федеральными законами; 12) составляющих информацию о состоянии окружающей среды (экологическую информацию). Статья 6. Предоставление информации, составляющей коммерческую тайну 1. Обладатель информации, составляющей коммерческую тайну, по мотивированному требованию органа государственной власти, иного государственного органа, органа местного самоуправления предоставляет им на безвозмездной основе информацию, составляющую коммерческую тайну. Мотивированное требование должно быть подписано уполномоченным должностным лицом, содержать указание цели и правового основания затребования информации, составляющей коммерческую тайну, и срок предоставления этой информации, если иное не установлено федеральными законами. 2. В случае отказа обладателя информации, составляющей коммерческую тайну, предоставить ее органу государственной власти, иному государственному органу, органу местного самоуправления данные органы вправе затребовать эту информацию в судебном порядке. 3. Обладатель информации, составляющей коммерческую тайну, а также органы государственной власти, иные государственные органы, органы местного самоуправления, получившие такую информацию в соответствии с частью 1 настоящей статьи, обязаны предоставить эту информацию по запросу судов, органов предварительного следствия, органов дознания по делам, находящимся в их производстве, в порядке и на основаниях, которые предусмотрены законодательством Российской Федерации. 3.1. Федеральные органы исполнительной власти, получившие в рамках установленных полномочий информацию, составляющую коммерческую тайну, от обладателя такой информации, предоставляют такую информацию по запросу федеральных органов исполнительной власти в рамках межведомственного информационного взаимодействия с соблюдением требований и (или) ограничений, установленных
perturbation.justification: The original text has a strong obligation to keep information confidential, but the changed text weakens this obligation by using subjective language ('will endeavor to treat as confidential...subjectively deems'). This introduces ambiguity and makes it less clear what information the company is actually required to keep confidential. The Law Explanation highlights that Federal Law No. 98-FZ requires definite standards for protecting trade secrets, and the change to 'subjectively deems' creates a discretionary aspect that goes against the law's necessity to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2 further supports this, indicating that commercial secrets are subject to legal regulation and restrictions, and the subjective determination undermines this regulatory framework by making the company's discretion the primary factor, rather than adhering to defined legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: The Company undertakes to take all reasonable measures for the purpose of preventing the unintentional disclosure of the Confidential Information. The Company undertakes to report attempts by unauthorized persons to obtain the Confidential Information, the loss or shortfall in the media containing such information, and also other facts that could impair the activities of the Client, and also attempts by third parties to use or disclose the Confidential Information within 2 (Two) working days of the actual receipt of the information on these attempts.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall consider implementing measures to possibly prevent unintentional disclosure of confidential information and may report potential security breaches within a reasonable timeframe, provided it does not disrupt business operations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly outlines the steps required to prevent confidential information from being disclosed and what to report and when. This has been replaced with softer language like 'consider implementing measures to possibly prevent' which does not enforce a strong undertaking, but rather gives it as an option. The two day window to report potential breeches is replaced with 'within a reasonable timeframe, provided it does not disrupt business operations' which gives the company discretion and does not reinforce or mandate any definitive action.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Law No. 152-FZ of July 27, 2006, on Personal Data (Russia)
perturbation.law_citation: Article 19. Requirements for ensuring the protection of personal data during their processing
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts Article 19 of the law on personal data protection in Russia, which mandates specific and concrete measures to ensure the security of personal data. By making these measures discretionary and allowing for delayed reporting based on business disruption, the modified text fails to meet the legal requirements for data protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 19 GDPR – Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16 , Article 17 (1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. 2 The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it. Suitable Recitals ( 66 ) Right to be Forgotten ← Art. 18 GDPR Art. 20 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction. The original contract mandated specific actions (e.g., reporting attempts within 2 days), while the changed text uses discretionary language ('consider implementing measures to possibly prevent') and vague timelines ('reasonable timeframe'), effectively undermining the original obligation. The Law Explanation states this change contradicts Article 19 of the law on personal data protection in Russia, which mandates specific and concrete measures. The changed text provides more business flexibility, but lessens the legal protections of the confidential information, setting up conditions where obligations may not be fully followed.

perturbation.original_text: The Company should protect the Confidential Information for five years since the Commencement Date of the Provision of the Confidential Information, or (if the Project lasts more than one year) for five years after the completion of the Project. In addition, the Clie nt shall have the right to indicate to The Company in writing any Confidential Information that it deems a trade secret. In this instance The Company undertakes to honor the confidentiality of said Confidential Information for an unlimited term or at the v ery least for as long as such Confidential Information remains a trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company should aim to protect Confidential Information for approximately five years, unless circumstances suggest otherwise. If the Client indicates any Confidential Information as a trade secret, The Company will attempt to honor the confidentiality of such information for as long as reasonably possible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text dictates the timelines for the protection of confidential information, including how trade secrets shall be protected and for how long. The changed text loosens the obligation by changing 'five years' to 'approximately five years, unless circumstances suggest otherwise' and 'unlimited term' to 'attempt to honor the confidentiality...for as long as reasonably possible'. The word 'aim' also softens the company's legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Civil Code of the Russian Federation, Part Four, Article 1467
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1467. Term of Exclusivity of Trade Secret of Production
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1467 dictates that the exclusivity of a trade secret lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret. By stating that the company will 'attempt' to honor confidentiality for as long as 'reasonably possible', the modified text introduces uncertainty and weakens the legally mandated protection of trade secrets.

IP Laws, Treaties & Judgments WIPO Standards IP Statistics WIPO Pearl (Terminology) WIPO Publications Country IP Profiles WIPO Knowledge Center WIPO Technology Trends Global Innovation Index World Intellectual Property Report PCT – The International Patent System ePCT Budapest – The International Microorganism Deposit System Madrid – The International Trademark System eMadrid Article 6ter (armorial bearings, flags, state emblems) Hague – The International Design System eHague Lisbon – The International System of Appellations of Origin and Geographical Indications eLisbon UPOV PRISMA Mediation Arbitration Expert Determination Domain Name Disputes Centralized Access to Search and Examination (CASE) Digital Access Service (DAS) WIPO Pay Current Account at WIPO WIPO Assemblies Standing Committees Calendar of Meetings WIPO Webcast WIPO Official Documents Development Agenda Technical Assistance IP Training Institutions COVID-19 Support National IP Strategies Policy & Legislative Advice Cooperation Hub Technology and Innovation Support Centers (TISC) Technology Transfer Inventor Assistance Program WIPO GREEN WIPO's Pat-INFORMED Accessible Books Consortium WIPO for Creators WIPO Translate Speech-to-Text Classification Assistant Member States Observers Director General Activities by Unit External Offices Staff Positions Non-staff Positions Procurement Results & Budget Financial Reporting Oversight On this page In action Laws and Treaties Related links Trade Secrets What is a trade secret? Trade secrets are intellectual property (IP) rights on confidential information which may be sold or licensed. What qualifies as a trade secret? In general, to qualify as a trade secret, the information must be: commercially valuable because it is secret, be known only to a limited group of persons , and be subject to reasonable steps taken by the rightful holder of the information to keep it secret, including the use of confidentiality agreements for business partners and employees. The unauthorized acquisition, use or disclosure of such secret information in a manner contrary to honest commercial practices by others is regarded as an unfair practice and a violation of the trade secret protection. What kind of inform
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, particularly trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates specific timelines for protection, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'approximately five years' and 'attempt to honor...for as long as reasonably possible.' This contradicts the principle that trade secrets should be protected as long as they remain secret and commercially valuable, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'Trade secrets are intellectual property (IP) rights on confidential information which may be sold or licensed... the information must be: commercially valuable because it is secret...subject to reasonable steps taken by the rightful holder of the information to keep it secret.' The modified text introduces uncertainty, making it difficult to enforce the protection of trade secrets, because the obligation for The Company to protect this information has been softened. Therefore, the change introduces a contradiction with respect to trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: Consequently, in the event of the disclosure of information related to the Confidential Information, THE COMPANY undertakes liability for any real damage suffered by the Client, and occurred directly by disclosure of Confidential Information on behalf of THE COMPAN Y in violation of this Agreement, however, the amount of reimbursement will be defined in accordance with the Consulting Services Agreement , in relation to which Confidential Information was provided by the Client. Under no circumstances THE COMPANY shall reimburse the Client against lost profit and consequential damages. In order to receive reimbursement of real damage, suffered by the Client in connection to this Agreement, the Client shall provide THE COMPANY with documents and/or other proof, confirming the presence and amount of real damage.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of the disclosure of information related to the Confidential Information, THE COMPANY may be liable for some of the damage suffered by the Client, if such damage is proven to be a direct result of a deliberate action by THE COMPANY, and to the extent outlined in the Consulting Services Agreement. THE COMPANY will not be liable for any lost profit or consequential damages. The Client should provide reasonable evidence of any damage to THE COMPANY for consideration.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the company 'undertakes liability for any real damage'. This is a clear undertaking which is now replaced with 'may be liable for some of the damage if such damage is proven to be a direct result of a deliberate action by THE COMPANY' This change now requires the Client to prove a 'deliberate action' which may be difficult if the actions were unintentional. The original text requires the client to provide 'documents and/or other proof, confirming the presence and amount of real damage' and the modified text reduces this requirement to 'reasonable evidence of any damage to THE COMPANY for consideration'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Civil Code of the Russian Federation, Article 15
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 15 allows for the recovery of losses caused by a violation of rights. The modified text introduces more difficult conditions for claiming damages (proving 'deliberate action'), contradicting the principle that damages resulting from a violation of rights, even unintentional ones, should be compensated.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-711. Buyer's Remedies in General; Buyer's Security Interest in Rejected Goods. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-711. Buyer's Remedies in General; Buyer's Security Interest in Rejected Goods. (1) Where the seller fails to make delivery or repudiates or the buyer rightfully rejects or justifiably revokes acceptance then with respect to any goods involved, and with respect to the whole if the breach goes to the whole contract (Section 2-612 ), the buyer may cancel and whether or not he has done so may in addition to recovering so much of the price as has been paid (a) "cover" and have damages under the next section as to all the goods affected whether or not they have been identified to the contract ; or (b) recover damages for non-delivery as provided in this Article (Section 2-713 ). (2) Where the seller fails to deliver or repudiates the buyer may also (a) if the goods have been identified recover them as provided in this Article (Section 2-502 ); or (b) in a proper case obtain specific performance or replevy the goods as provided in this Article (Section 2-716 ). (3) On rightful rejection or justifiable revocation of acceptance a buyer has a security interest in goods in his possession or control for any payments made on their price and any expenses reasonably incurred in their inspection, receipt , transportation, care and custody and may hold such goods and resell them in like manner as an aggrieved seller (Section 2-706 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract text states that 'THE COMPANY undertakes liability for any real damage suffered by the Client'. The modified text changes this to 'THE COMPANY may be liable for some of the damage suffered by the Client'. This weakens the obligation of the company to be liable. Furthermore, it introduces the requirement of proving 'deliberate action' by the company. The Law Explanation states that Article 15 allows for the recovery of losses caused by a violation of rights and that damages resulting from a violation of rights, even unintentional ones, should be compensated. While the provided snippet 1 (§ 2-711. Buyer's Remedies in General) discusses buyer's remedies and security interests, it does not directly address liability for damages. However, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that requiring proof of 'deliberate action' contradicts the principle of compensating for damages even if unintentional. The scraped snippet 1, although not directly on point, illustrates the broader principle that remedies are available when a contract is breached or rights are violated. The modification, therefore, introduces a hurdle to recovery not present in the original agreement and potentially conflicts with the broader legal principle of compensating for damages caused by a violation of rights.
